content: The manual, Antora and so...

Let's see if we can handle open issues here. One basic is the development manual vs user manual and how the plugins fits into this scheme.

Rick: I know you have thoughts on this...
good night...

Plugin User Documentation
Last count was 41-42 Plugins, not including Beta Plugins and there are 10-12 of those.
Beta Documentation can stay on the Dokuwiki untill the author decides to move up to regular.
The 40 regular plugins all have Dokuwiki User Documentation.
We have been bloating the User Manual with these, but would like to have them distributed with the plugin.
This will reduce the size of the User Manual distribution quite a lot. I studied this earlier but don't remember the figures.
I was hoping we could get a process to do this to encourage plugin devs to start migrating as I think it is going to take awhile to get all the plugins done.
Nite.

Also there are a number of images that are stored in the Dev Wiki.
A number of these images may no longer be used by links in the documentation. A number of them should probably be dropped if they are not really needed for understanding to reduce the size.

If I were to collect all the images into one folder, is there a way to determine if they are still used by the documents and needed, so that I can delete the others?
The problem is partly due to the images being helter skelter in various media directories in the wiki.

Copied from github issues:

Alec wrote

Which was caused by the plugins manuals being included in the dokuwiki developer manual. Up to now, I have been porting it more or less mechanically, but you're right. It's time to change that.

Those plugins are Beta Plugins, about 10-13 of them.

I hope you do not intend to include all 41-42 normal plugins in the User Documentation. It is a sizeable chunk of data and media. It should be distributed individually for offline use to reduce the overall size of the User Manual when distributed for offline use. When the User Manual is on-line, there could be a page with external links to each of the plugins output documentation (html or pdf) where ever that is required to reside or when the plugin is updated, the right format documentation could land in a dedicated spot for the online manual.

I also think the plugin antora documents directory should reside in the github.com/[user]/[plugin_pi]/"manual" directory (is fine for me) so that the antora commands can include the right format output for off line use, with each tarball. There may be a need to have output also go to some dedicated location to update the on line User Manual and some other location for offline to get into the tarball.

Just some thoughts here. I think html is the right format for online documentation. It could also be used for offline. I know that we used to use html for the User Manual some years ago, then went with pdf and epub because it got edited manually to reduce the size and it was the easiest way to deal with it.

Note that the Android version has a separate Android App for documentation, I think that is in pdf but I am not sure. Perhaps that is why Dave said pdf?
I already have some plugin stuff sorted. Would ocpn_plugins_manual be a suitable title to work with?
why not? But we need Rick's opinion as well.
I read your message as that you think the overall idea is good?

Alec,
Is there going to be a

Developer Manual Component

- How to build and program for OpenCPN
- Setup of dev environment on various OS
- Managed Plugin System
- Package Plugin System
- API and other essentials

Plugin Developer Manual Component

- How to build and program for Plugins
- Managed Plugins - how to setup and build them
- Packaged Plugins - how to setup and build them
- Linked List of regular plugins
- (eventually to antora components in github.com/[usr]/[plugin]/manual/
- Linked List of Beta Plugins
- going to dokuwiki new "Beta Plugins" Book (I will move them all there)

Plugin Manual Component(s)

- in each github.com plugin repository maintained by owner.

..as different antora components? Is that where this is headed?

looks good to me, but for now I think the idea is that there should be

A User's Manual
A Developer Manual
And a Users's plugin manual

Obviously, we need th handle plugin development in the DM, but this is as I see it a later issue. The important point is having three manuals instead of two. I
... and if we can agree on these three manuals. That could be a starting point

Yes

  • User Manual- Basic
  • User Manual -Advanced
  • User Plugin Manual
  • Developer Manual (includes plugin development)

Yes, but I have a question. Where do Beta Plugins go?
They don't really belong in the User Manual until the final stage where they are being used regularly and tested.
See the three on the opencpn.org website that are more public and being tested?
https://opencpn.org/OpenCPN/info/downloadplugins.html
These and perhaps a few others could go under a "Beta" category in the User Plugin Manual, but not the others, I think.
So where should they be kept?

hm... I can some options. A separate manual beta-plugins? Two versions of the plugin manual, one of which containing also the beta plugins (in a separate section)? I guess we can find out something...
But then again, it seems like we all agree that a separate User's Plugin Manual is an idea worth exploring. @Mike Rossiter : Did you say that you could be able to look into it?

Yes, already started looking at this. ocpn_user_plugins_manual? Perhaps get the main plugins sorted and then think about the betas later. I can add adocs to my plugins. Perhaps I could send Rick adocs for his. Rick... could you put these in a manual/modules/ROOT/pages etc folder? Standard layout like https://github.com/Rasbats/managed_plugins/tree/asciidoc but with docs changed to manual.
At some point we are going to have to call out to developers to help with the documentation. Reaction?

What we need is a playbook directory, something similar to ocpn-manuals but for the plugins.

You might be interested in .github/workflows/main.yml in ocpn-manuals. It's a github action which automatically rebuilds and publishes the result on new commits, in my case on leamas.github.io://ocpn-manuals/. I think you can use it more or less verbatim, but note that as-is it uses the gh-pages branch.

[out of sync] Yes we will need to do that. The first step would be that we made one or two PR's against existing plugins. During that process we will see what the difficulties are and document them. After that. it's a question of time...
@Mike Rossiter Did I say "Great" for handling this task? No, I didn't. Shame on me. Because, it is indeed great...

You guys are moving this along, it is wonderful! Just a thought , shorten names? too much typing.
maybe o-pi-manuals? or is ocpn a standard? could the ocpn-manuals become o-manuals? Just wondering

@Mike Rossiter @Rick Gleason One basic question. Who are we writing for? I tend to think that a Developer Manual is written for an audience with programming skills. But this contradicts the current introduction, so we need to talk about it.

My proposal: The Developer Manual targets readers with basic programming skills. Or?

I think a better place for the introduction would be in the User Manual. But OpenCPN is a great learning tool (I have found!) and we should not discourage people from trying stuff. They may not even want to call themselves developers.

You are writing for Volunteers who have some programming experience.
Most of the current devs have programmed, but perhaps years ago, may have retired and want to contribute.
Some are self starters with good current skills and current knowledge about tools, but some are rusty and need more guidance.

I suggest that it would be best to assume that some guidance getting "setup" for various things is going to be a huge help in attracting talent and helping new devs manage the complex process.

Alec your skills and expectations about programming are quite high. For me it is still a struggle because it is not Pascal. So there is a wide range, not one level.

Jon wrote: including a section on local builds using these environments. I now use...
Local Building would be a very good topic in the plugin manual.

I'd be lost without being able to build locally, due to my level of skill, and I think such a section or component would be hugely helpful.

I have found using bash locally as you have shown and setup is essential. Not all starting plugin devs will know about or have this knowledge, and helping them out getting setup is really the first step for the plugin part of the manual.

Right, I also think we should be welcoming. But the core issue is that we cannot have everything everywhere, and this means that information which clearly is aimed to for example testers or translators shouldn't dwell in a Developers Manual. Because the will most likely not read it at all
Working with a mockup. Here, there is a temporary section called User Manual we can place things we think belongs there until we have a better way to not lose it.l

Yes, that's a perfectly reasonable point. The OpenCPN support "Community" is very broad. It's the only way to get everything done. This is written in the reverse order, purposely to encourage others to progress and get more involved.

1.Participate and Answer questions on CF.

  1. Language Translator
  2. Editor of Opencpn Manual
  3. Manage Website, Tracker, Wiki, Antora

OpenCPN Development Team

  1. Alpha Test OpenCPN
  2. Beta Test OpenCPN
  3. Submit PR to fix, improve, change
  4. Reorganize and improve OpenCPN builds. (Leamas)
  5. Design and implement a new system like PI Manager (Leamas)

Plugin Development Team

  1. Alpha Test plugins
  2. Beta Test plugins
  3. Submit PR's to fix, update or improve plugins
  4. Maintain and update Plugins
  5. Develop Plugins

I have seen people get involved at the Editor level and work up to building a plugin and it is fun to watch provide guidance and help where possible, though I am not that technical programming. I can suggest resources.

The point is, that this is a very broad Community with lots of talent from many industries and endeavors. It would be best to support that as much as possible with documentation that succinctly addresses the hurdles and is cogent and concise to get the job done. I can only say, that it is best not to create hurdles that are unnecessary. Keep in mind that we are not all as accomplished as you, Pavel and bdbcat!
@rgleason: Sure. But since this indeed is addressing all sorts of users, not only developers, the place for this is the User's Manual (UM), right?

First mockup iteration is available at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/. We are still discussing the first chapter. Note that I have resolved the Plugin CI/Deploy issues in the standard system design way i. e., postponing it to later :)

8:16 AM

Alec I think the target is perhaps a little broader, including testers? , but certainly they have to be able to read code and do some programming. (I regard myself in this category as I am not really a Programmer). I think the majority are Programmers who are also learning, but there are a healthy number of experts too. Systems like Internationalization and setup of the necessary programs is certainly a necessary reference page in this technical manual, just as explaining how to setup for Plugin Manager is needed.

I think the target does not include the first group I listed : CF suppor, Language translator (exception being the page to get setup), OpenCPN Manual editor, and managing website, tracker, dokuwiki and antora. Dokuwiki has its own Editors Manual.

Where would instructions to get setup and use asciidoc/antora documentation get put?

Problem is that a lot of people which could take part as for example testers or translators might not even look into the Developers Manual, disturbed by the very title. So we should have a huge, warm welcome to everybody in all kinds of roles, more or less as you have written today. But that belongs to the User Manual, which no-one is afraid of.
For the Developers Manual I think keeping a focus on contributing code might make things easier.
@Rick Gleason @Rick Gleason Thoughts?

I see your point, but I think that Programmers need to be reminded of this too. This is a voluntary environment and there is a big diverse range of talent.
How can we keep everyone working together, and not seem like a fortress. I wrote that for a specific purpose in response to Pavel at one point, who had a "Black and White" view. I think a brief paragraph at the beginning would be good, the rest of it perhaps does belong in the User Manual. I will write that for you. (Smile)
Absolutely, and for this reason we should link back from the Developers Manual (DM) to the User Manual (UM) when talking about the community
If you write someting, first look into https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals so you get some context
I think you are right, Link from DM to UM is good. Some good content & rewriting there. How do we do this?

If you look into the mockup I have, there is a new Heading User Manual. My idea is that stuff which should be moved from Antors DM to UM is placed there. And, TBH, that you handles the task to transfer it. It's probably easier to use the current dokuwiki DM pages and move to UM than to use the asciidoc pages, I guess.

Basically, updating UM your show. OK?

So you are keeping https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/ocpn-dev-manual/5.3.1/Community.html - I think it is good.
"This manual is about contributing to code"" -- A clear goal.

Yes, I will update UM

I like this arrangement with the "Old Dev Manual" being there for reference. It will smooth the transition.
As things get rewritten, shouldn't we cull the old pages if they are redundent?
Perhaps keeping how we compiled version 4.8 might be useful to someone,
but If we improve and rewrite a page like Internationalization and it is better, we should just remove that page in the old Dev Manual?
I'll move the old dev manual to the User Manual heading. We could consider this a queue for stuff which should be moved to the UM. When you are done with that, we just drop the corresponding entries.

Alec, You've changed the plan. I am not going to be storing some of that stuff in User Manual! Its too big already and has to be totally rewritten.

This is clearly Old Dev stuff. Let it hang around until it is rewritten and organized for the Dev Manual. Some of it should be retained I think.
I really appreciate your judgment, I havn't really looked that much int this. Still it's seems wise to place what we dont include visible until we make the final decision to drop it, right?
We can of course also place it in the Attic

Yes! That's the right idea. Maybe we move the Old Dev stuff into the Attic as it is "Rewritten".

Attic

  • "Redundant - to be removed later" Page has been rewritten and this is being retained for reference for just awhile.
  • "Reference " -indexed by OpenCPN Version - kept for programmers building and maintaining old versions, environments and systems.

You don't want this stuff in the DM as it confuses, and I don't want it in UM because it is not germaine, but it does have use to old programmers.

I have updated the mockup at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals. The two pages I have removed/heavily changed is there.
OK?
Eventually, we'll have to clean up the Attic. Sort of sounds familiar.

That's fine. When edited and in DM, move Old Manual --> Attic

Attic

  • Redundent
  • Reference
  • UM

Beginnings are hard. But we are moving, and this topic can be closed

Note how elegant zulip is. One can back-track, and change the topic for messages

Somehow I found this and ended up there in trying to add a page
https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/AlternativeWorkflow/index.html

I think Mike is working on this and it looks good.
I do think that "Alternative Managed Workflow for OpenCPN Plugins" is a mouthful.
Why not something like

Alternative Plugin Workflow

  • Managed - Shipdriver
  • Managed - Testplugin

What is the base Plugin Workflow? maybe there should be a namespace for that too?
Yes, "Alternative workflow" needs to be replaced. Alternative to what, one might wonder...

I have always thought that

Plugin Workflow

  • Package Installer (original process) Create exe, deb, pkg file that User has to locate, download and execute to install while OpenCPN is closed.
  • Managed Plugin (Leamas process) Tarballs to the cloud with metadata links to the tarballs available within the Opencpn Plugin UI
    + Shipdriver Alternative (Frontend1)
    + Testplugin Frontend2
Maybe "Packaged Installer" and "Managed Installer" to make it parallel

How would I get an up to date ocpn-dev-manual? Do I make a PR from your repos and then merge it?
Then I can add that page and push back to you?

workflow (gough)
workflow(leamas)

Too many Frontends. And it recognises the work of each programmer.

ShipDriver-leamas
Testplugin-gough

To help other developers in their own builds.
or just call it testplugin (which is the name used, nothing wrong in that), and call the other "shipdriver". KISS..
Yes, kiss either way , with/without authors
How are we supposed to work here using git?? Do I just make a PR from leamas and merge it?

Nothing to compare. I guess it stays current somehow with antora --fetch?
https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual/compare/main...leamas:main

Same thing with Mike's
https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual/compare/main...Rasbats:main

hm... I have just a mockup at this poimt, and it lives in the branch mockup. You can fetch that branch if you want:

git remote add upstream https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-dev-manual.git
git remote update upstream
git fetch upstream mocklup:mockup

But the simplest would be to just clone https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-dev-manual.git, and checkout the mockup branch
This is not working right. I have no ocpn-manuals remote repository!
I already have ocpn-dev-manual in my remote. I think I might try a PR from that branch, or perhaps I should make another branch mockup and get it.
I followed your suggestions. Now I need to change the branch in the ymls I think.

Yes, I simply changed the branch from main to mockup
Then antora bldloc.yml and it appeared in the refreshed browser.
Found whitespace at the end of one yml sentence. Could be the problem.
We need to organize this in a better way, for sure. What are trying to achieve, really?
Get connected properly

My vote

Plugin Workflow

  • Package Installer
  • Managed Installer
    + Shipdriver -Leamas -Rossiter
    + TestPlugin - Gough

Managed Installer
Shipdriver documents have good common documentation for setup

Common Setup

  • Metadata Files
  • Appveyor
  • CircleCI
  • Cloudsmith
  • Travis

ShipDriver

  • Github
  • Adapting the Plugin
  • Code changes

Testplugin

  • Github
  • Adapting the Plugin
  • Code changes

Additional Support

  • Build Locally
  • Useful Information
  • Plugin Dependencies
Connected, in the sense that you can reproduce https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-dev-manual.git? Or?

I have pulled the stuff I think belong to OpenCPN Development into the chapter, and in the process moved some stuff to the Attic. Basically, this means that remaining stuff in Old Manual is the Plugins stuff. Available at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/.

@Rick Gleason: Anything I missed?
To just get this chapter into shape will take time. I have fixed the linux compilation page as a starter.

Perhaps I see what you mean. It's really I who is disconnected, since I have cloned rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual and works in that clone without making a PR to rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual which is the upstream. I could certainly do such a PR if you want me to.
Made a PR. This should make you up to date for now.

Alec, I have added the two Compile Windows Community 2013 pages and edited them to work properly using your 'mockup' branch

I have added these files and made a PR.
Now should I push my PR up to remote and make a Pull Request to your repos?
Or should I do a rebase because you have made changes?
Remember, you are upstream, you have the master copy. So you don't make PRs., you accept them. And if you want to change ocpn-dev-manual, you just change it (but don't forget to push).
My PR might run into trouble if/when you push changes, but that's my problem.
That said, IMHO 2013 stuff belongs in the attic

Alec,. this morning you made a PR 1 Mockup to ocpn-dev-manual my remote branch "main". It looks good.
I have been working locally on a branch "mockup" taken from your repos. and have made a Commit. I need to flatten the attachments folder and change some links then make another Commit.

I could accept your merge and bring those changes local.
Then push my mockup changes up to my remote "main" and then pull the changes down into my local main?
Or is there a better way?

"That said, IMHO 2013 stuff belongs in the attic"
Yes, that's fine with me. Lets keep an Attic "Reference" folder for old stuff that we want to keep available for old programmers on old machines. IE don't discard old compile and build stuff, it may be useful to someone.

The other folder in Attic would be "Redundant" for old files that have been improved and replicated and are headed for the trash.

"Old Manual" will eventually disappear as necessary pages get replicated and moved into the new manual or moved to "Attic - Redundant" or "Attic - Reference"

Then we can do your unused image script to remove that deadweight.

There are a lot of changes in that, Alec. I want to get my changes in there too, but I think I should add my changes first to the "main" branch because my changes which add two files to old manual and some images, which are based on your mockup. Doing it the other way will end up with lots of conflicts I think. Then i think
you'll need to rebase?

Yes, that's the problem with being downstream :(

Don't worry, I'll find a way. Merge your changes push, and let my PR break. It's as it should.
Don't accept my merge. Instead, push your changes so I can rebase.
Done now

Alec you removed the old Plugin API Document entirely.
There is valuable information in there that should be left in the attic.
Please copy files the file and edit that, leaving the documents in the Old Plugin. Thanks.

Alex I merged. I realize that what we should probably do is rename the old files with "-old.adoc" and then just copy it and change the nav.adoc references accordingly.
I'll retrieve that one, because old programmers with old hardware will need the API versions and sequence. We'll just leave it in the nav.doc Attic Reference.
Incidently, is it possible to NOT include all of "Attic" whenever the Dev Manual is included in software?

Alec, I changed nav.adoc to add subfolders for Attic. Made a PR back to your mockup-1
I would be good if docs were renamed xxxx-old.adoc and then copied for new work!!

I think we've lost old linux and old mac and maybe old windows build instructions , and the api listing for compile of old versions.
If someone goes back to a 4.8.8 commit, then the instructions change back, so we need the old docs.

I have a choice, I guess.

  1. Forget it or copy and translate the ddokuwiki docs run through pandoc and then edit
  2. Find them somewhere in an earlier commit.
  3. Leave it on Dokuwiki and have a Dev Manual - Old. Which I would rather not do.

Again: you are upstream, and yo don't need to (that is, shouldn't ) do any PR in this project. Just change the main branch, that's your task. My task is to keep up with your changes.

The mockup-1 branch which I used for the PR is a topic branch. After that you have merged it into main (accepted the PR) I will eventually delete it. It serves no purpose. The same is true for any PR.

Our common task is to develop the main branch, so the only question is the state of that. I'll know when I rebase locally if you have pushed your changes to main.
OK, I will follow this etiquette. Is there somewhere I can refer to this etiquette or is it just kept in programmers DNA?
https://opencpn.org/wiki/dokuwiki/doku.php?id=opencpn:developer_manual:developer_guide:internationalization:plugin_languages
It's in the DNA ;I Actually, you will find it any git primer out there. I think your problem is just that you are upstream, as a newbie this isn't that common
I get it, but git PR does work both ways functionally, and it is perhaps a little friendlier signaling a change, but I will follow etiquette.

It's the KISS principle, again. A PR is somehow a work around needed when one don't have write access, and thus must request she who has to make some changes. So, no need to make a PR if you are the maintainer.

Technically, I could just merge some changes. But by making you, the owner of the repo, make all changes we make everything pass through a common point (you) which eventually can create some sort of order. If I just merged stuff I might force to rebase which isn't nice.

@Mike Rossiter @Rick Gleason

There is a new version of the DM at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/. Still a loooong way from completed, but:

  • I think the basic structure is workable (?)
  • There is a new, customized UI bundle
  • The editing stuff is activated, pages can be fixed by just clicking "Edit this page"

The version above is ahead of current ocpn-dev-manual, I'm waiting for if/when Rick has time to accept/reject/comment on https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-dev-manual/pull/45.

Here is also https://github.com/Rasbats/managed_plugins/pull/3, same situation: Waiting for feedback. This is less pressing, though
The biggest hole in the current state is probably the page(s) on testplugin/testplugin2. Also, this is something I just cannot do, it's above my paygrade.
I have been updating watchdog to v1.0160 and taking notes for this purpose.

I have an issue that needs to be sorted.
I have run locally antora bld-loc.yml on both ocpn-manuals and ocpn-dev-manuals
There are a bunch of files that have changed in both directories.
I have not merged the changes. So now what?
There are two choices
A. On ocpn-dev-manual git checkout-b master-bkup and then delete master and git pull.
B. On ocpn-dev-manual turn the changes into a commit and git rebase and solve the conflicts.
Same on ocpn-manuals (but that is your repos ) so I should follow.

I fixed my ocpn-manuals by following helpful git history link rasbats sent

  1. switching remote names leamas = upstream rgleason=origin
  2. git merge --abort and then git checkout -b main-bkup to save my changes
  3. git checkout hash to go back to.
  4. git checkout -b main-bkup
  5. git branch -D main
  6. git checkout -b main

Now I have to figure out ocpn-dev-manuals
Of course I lost all my changes. I don't think very much of this "system", it is very cumbersome and intuitive. And I don't really like it so far.
I think the changes are now put back. Alec, I shouldn't push this at all should I?
ocpn-dev-manual worked out well, just needed to pull the merges down to local and there were no conflicts.

One thing could be said about git which is sure: you never actually lose something, it's still there. Problem is just how to find it.... Making a tag before starting adventures like that is a god habit, the tag serves as backup.

If you have problems with ocpn-dev-manual, the only thing I can think of is that you merged my changes before you tried to merge yours. Which, have we said, is problematic since it forces you to rebase . Better then to merge your own changes first, and force me to rebase.

" I have run locally antora bld-loc.yml on both ocpn-manuals and ocpn-dev-manuals" .. and not merged them.

This will always be a problem. We are working on the same files, and makes different changes. This is actually not really a problem with git or Antora, it's the general problem when several people works in the same file(s). You are just not used to not being alone. The general strategy is to merge or make PRs often, so the delta is kept small. Some details:

For ocpn-dev-manuals (again): merge your own changes before merging my PR, pushing the problem to me.

For ocpn-manuals: As soon as you have done anything, make a PR, see that I merge it and then rebase you own copy on leamas/ocpn-manuals. This way, we keep also them in sync. As long as there are no PR from you , there is no way we can keep changes in sync.

For your local build files: put them in another branch, or just not put them under git control.

ocpn-manuals is too messy, agreed, and my plan to turn this upside-down so you are upstream also here. Just some touch first...
@Rick Gleason : I have rebuilt the manual using updated sources in ocpn-manuals.yml. Available at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals

@Rick Gleason : We have things we have decided should go into the User's Manual (UM), like the "welcome to the community" stuff. But: how should we track this? There is obviously no github repo for the UM where we can file issues. Thoughts?

Alec
You and Mike have taken content originally developed in dokuwiki that is "COMMON" to both examples and placed it in the isolated remote repository for Shipdriver. This content should be in the main opencpn-dev-manual, so that it can apply to both plugin templates. Otherwise I will just have to duplicate it, and I will do so.

Your current wiki is not current.

Re topic above. What is the hidden agenda? Tell me what you think.
I will move them there when I get done with testplugin.
I have no hidden agenda, for sure. Could you please provide examples? I agree that stuff which is not specific to shipdriver or testplugin should live in ocpn-dev-manual

Today I have made some pm-overview files that should handle this OK, could probably have some improvement but it is pretty consolidated and compact.. Push up to repos and merged your changes too.

Sorry, I got up on the wrong side of the bed. Knowing it would be impossible to finish all this today. Hopefully tomorrow.

I'm looking into the new stuff you have have added (and plan to add) for testplugin. One problem is that things are asymmetric: shipdriver lives in a separate manual whereas testplugin is part of the main manual.

@Rick Gleason : I can see advantages in breaking out the testplugin stuff into a separate manual. Can you?
What are they? This is comfortable and working right now and I want to finish it. Preferably today, but that is a real long shot.

well... if you are about to finish this today, do that.

If we move the testplugin to a separate manual, this would create a clear division between the overall stuff and testplugin. As it is now, it's IMHO a bit hard to see the boundaries. for both readers and authors.

Let's talk about it when you're finished. It 's no big deal to move the pages to a separate repo, should we decide that.
It will not be finished today. Got embroiled cleaning up watchdog.
This will take a long time anyway. It's certainly not a question of days.
@Rick Gleason : Have you added all files you intend to add, besides possible new files under Testplugin Template?
No, and I want to keep testplugin where it is.

I've got other things I have to accomplish, so this is going to take back seat and be a filler. It is spring. I have 2+ weeks of work on the boat and I have the cupola to finish painting and varnishing and I have 8 shrubs in the garden to transplant NOW. Plus I have to drive to Chicago to see my aging parent in laws (96 & 97) who we have not seen for over a year. That will take a week (I can work on it more during that time). First: let's conclude that there is no additional build dependencies when making a legacy .deb package in the general case.

Is this then about a specific plugin and it's requirements. In other words: could you complete make tarball before trying to make a .deb package? If not, this is just about a strange plugin. Plugins will always have different dependencies, both in build time (manageable) and in runtime (tricky).

When I build shipdriver_pi@3d5c67c it builds out of the box on Windows.

However, as documented one needs to run buildwin\win-deps.bat first -- it not only install dependencies, it also sets up the environment. If not, the build fails on missing wxWidgets.
My mistake. I should have remembered. But to keep it simple for the demo is my fix possible, or does this cause a problem with other OS?
Having os-specific quirks is IMHO the opposite of keeping it simple.
OTOH, since even you make this mistake: Should we add a check in cmake which, on windows, detects that the environment is missing and writes a warning message?
Why os-specific if it works for all os? Have your tried this mod? But yes a warning is needed.

We have put some effort in this build model and verified it on all sorts of hosts. For me, it seems we should stick to it, focusing on the rough corners such as the missing warning on Windows.

IOW: changing this carries a high cost, and I don't see that the current situation motivates this change. Do you?

Also: by enforcing windows users to run win_deps.bat we automatically fix the build dependencies problem, script installs them if they are not in place. This makes for a simple model, a single script to rule them all. Not bad

The nuisance of running another batch file before I flash up my code I could do without. But your last point is a good one. I am already set up with all the dependencies. @Rick Gleason --- downloads as "opencpn-master-plugins" Why?
This is because there are now two components in opencpn-plugins-manual. One is "opencpn-master-plugins" the other is "opencpn-beta-plugins".
You have this in your build.yml:
branches: main
start_path: manual
It needs to be:
branches: main
start_paths: 

  - manual
  - manual-beta

Still a WIP but I now have the modules for the betas in place and ready for delegating.
